headline: Modi Says India Beat the Oil Crisis. Who Actually Paid the Bill?
subhead: The refineries absorbed the shock. The public balance sheet will absorb the cost.
about: Speaking at the inauguration of the HPCL Rajasthan Refinery at Balotra, the Prime Minister used the occasion to make a broad political claim: that India had come through the Iran conflict — an event that spiked crude and gas prices across the world — without the fuel rationing, LPG shortages and pump-price surges that hit other economies. His account has a specific mechanism in it. India's refineries increased throughput, and the public-sector oil marketing companies (the OMCs) absorbed the losses rather than passing them to consumers, so petrol, diesel and cooking-gas prices stayed flat even as global markets convulsed. He framed the panic that did circulate as "politically motivated rumour-mongering" that "failed." Underlying all of it: India kept buying discounted Russian and Iranian crude through the crisis, and diversified its sourcing across more than forty countries, which is what let the refiners keep the taps open. The Balotra refinery itself is presented as part of the answer — more domestic refining capacity so the country is less exposed next time.
left: Read the mechanism honestly and the "efficiency" claim inverts. "Public OMCs bearing the losses" is not the same as avoiding a cost — it means the state-owned oil companies swallowed the shock, and their margins are ultimately public money. The pain didn't vanish; it was moved off the retail price tag and onto public-sector balance sheets, where it stays invisible until the subsidy or recapitalisation bill eventually surfaces. So the government is claiming a clean win for something the exchequer quietly paid for. And crediting "diplomacy and strong decision-making" papers over the uncomfortable engine of the whole thing: India's dependence on cheap crude from sanctioned and pariah suppliers. That's opportunism — buying from whoever is cheapest because they're radioactive to everyone else — dressed up as strategic foresight. The rumour-mongering line is the tell: it pre-emptively delegitimises anyone who points at the hidden costs.
right: This is precisely what competent crisis management is supposed to look like, and the results are the proof. A billion-plus people were insulated from a genuine global energy shock — no rationing, no queues, no price spike — while wealthier, better-resourced economies flinched. That doesn't happen by accident; it happens through diversified sourcing, strategic reserves and a government willing to make the OMCs a shock-absorber for households. Buying discounted crude wherever it's available is not something to apologise for — it's elementary national interest, and every serious energy importer does the equivalent. The Balotra refinery is the forward-looking half of the same strategy: build the domestic capacity so the next shock is even easier to absorb. Calling the panic "rumour-mongering" is fair when the panic didn't materialise into actual shortages.
reality: India didn't dodge the shock so much as socialise it. The OMCs eating margin losses is a real cost — it's just been pushed off the pump price and onto the books of state-owned companies, where it will show up later as a subsidy line, a recapitalisation, or thinner public-sector profits, at which point the political credit will already have been banked. That's not a trick unique to this government; it's how energy-price shocks are routinely absorbed by import-dependent states, and it works precisely because the cost is deferred and diffuse. The genuinely durable achievement here is the sourcing diversification across forty-plus countries — that's structural resilience that survives the news cycle. The "no price rise, we managed it" framing is political packaging wrapped around what is, underneath, an accounting maneuver.

headline: Who Gets Deleted? The Quiet War Over India's Voter Rolls
subhead: An EC slogan, a Delhi conclave, and a hunger strike converge on the same question.
about: Three strands of the same fight ran across both papers. First, the Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls continues across Delhi and other states, with the Election Commission running full-page enumeration-form guides carrying the slogan "No Eligible Voter to be excluded, No Ineligible Person to be included" — an unusually defensive piece of public messaging for a routine administrative exercise. Second, a Delhi conclave of retired judges, former bureaucrats, lawyers and civil-society leaders — including former Chief Election Commissioner Ashok Lavasa — convened and rejected the "One Nation, One Election" proposal while explicitly questioning the SIR's process and timing, and pushing for a caste census. Third, the CJP hunger strike at Jantar Mantar, tied to Sonam Wangchuk's associate and demanding action, drew fresh backing from AAP, Congress and CPI(M), with a protester hospitalised. A separate news-analysis piece noted the EC can call bypolls any time within six months of a vacancy and is currently sitting on five vacant seats, feeding opposition complaints about selective "hurry and delay."
left: The revision is deletion-by-documentation — an exercise whose burden falls hardest on exactly the people least able to produce paperwork on demand: the poor, migrants, and minorities. The EC feeling compelled to plaster "no eligible voter excluded" across full-page ads is itself revealing; you don't advertise your neutrality that loudly unless it's under question. That retired judges, former CECs and constitutional experts are now publicly protesting is a measure of how far institutional trust has collapsed — these aren't street activists, they're the people who used to run the system. And the convergence of the entire opposition behind a hunger strike shows this has stopped being about clerical accuracy and become a democracy-integrity fight. The five vacant seats the EC won't fill, set against the intensity of the roll revision, is the pattern: move fast where it helps, drag your feet where it doesn't.
right: Accurate electoral rolls are the non-negotiable foundation of a clean election, and stripping out duplicate, dead and ineligible entries is basic hygiene that the opposition itself demanded for years. The loud "no eligible voter excluded" messaging is not defensiveness — it's transparency, the Commission openly signalling the safeguards built into the process. A conclave of retired officials and an opposition-backed hunger strike is manufactured outrage: the usual suspects building a pre-emptive alibi so that any electoral defeat can be reframed as theft rather than a verdict. Dragging former CECs out to lend gravitas doesn't change that these are political actors making a political case against an independent constitutional body simply because its work is inconvenient. On bypolls, the timing is a matter of administrative discretion the RPA explicitly allows, not a conspiracy.
reality: Roll revision is litigated only in states where the margins are thin — nobody fights it where they're winning comfortably. Whoever shapes who gets added and who gets dropped shapes the outcome in the fifty-odd seats that actually decide a national contest, and that is the real stake beneath all the "purity of the rolls" language on both sides. The retired-judges conclave and the multi-party hunger strike matter less for the specific merits of their arguments than for the signal they send: trust in the neutrality of the process — not just the results — has eroded to the point where the machinery itself is now the battleground. The five unfilled bypoll seats are the quiet illustration of the deeper truth: the timing of every electoral action is now read, by everyone, as tactical. The institution's credibility erodes whichever side turns out to be right this cycle, and that erosion is the durable cost.

headline: 80 'Terrorists' Named Without a Single Trial. The List Keeps Growing.
subhead: Six Indians, seventeen Pakistanis, and an engineer from Bengaluru — all branded by executive order.
about: The Centre made two overlapping moves. It added 23 individuals — including relatives of Hafiz Saeed and Lashkar-e-Taiba aides — to the official terror list under the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, taking the total number of individually designated terrorists in India to 80. Separately, the Ministry of Home Affairs designated six Indian nationals and 17 Pakistan nationals as terrorists, linking them to Jaish-e-Mohammed and Lashkar networks operating across the border, and to cross-border recruitment, arms trafficking, financing, and drone-based weapons delivery. Notably, an engineer originally from Bengaluru was among the 23. The government's framing is that these designations strengthen India's hand in dismantling terror networks at both the national and international level, and build the case for pressure on Pakistan.
left: Individual "terrorist" designation under UAPA is a peculiarly dangerous instrument because it carries no trial, and delisting is near-impossible. Labelling a person — including an Indian engineer from Bengaluru — is an executive act of branding that never has to survive the test of a courtroom. Expanding the list to 80 is easy to announce and hard to challenge, and the civil-liberties cost of designation-without-conviction is precisely the part that goes unexamined. When the state can mark someone a terrorist by administrative fiat, the burden of proof that protects the innocent simply drops out. That an Indian citizen is on the list should invite scrutiny about the threshold, not applause about the number.
right: Naming the architects and financiers of cross-border terror — Hafiz Saeed's network above all — is exactly the pre-emptive, intelligence-led disruption that stops attacks before they are executed, rather than counting bodies afterward. Designation is a tool for freezing assets, cutting off funding and building the documented case for international pressure on the state that shelters these networks. The inclusion of Indian nationals is the alarming part and vindicates the vigilance: radicalisation reaches inside the country, and pretending otherwise is the real danger. Squeamishness about the mechanism protects no one but the people it names.
reality: Individual designation is a low-cost, high-signal instrument — it lets the state take visible action and send a diplomatic message at the same time, without the burden of proof that a prosecution would demand, which is exactly why the list keeps growing to round numbers like 80. Whether any single name is a genuine operational node in a terror network or a convenient addition that strengthens a diplomatic narrative is, by the design of the tool, untestable from the outside. The pressure on Pakistan that the listing generates is as much the point as any concrete security gain — the designation does political and diplomatic work regardless of what a trial would have found, which is why the mechanism exists in the form it does.
imageCredit: Ministry of Home Affairs / Wikimedia Commons

headline: Holy Money, Missing Money: Inside the Ayodhya Donation Scandal
subhead: The RSS wants correction. Congress wants a Supreme Court probe. The money is still missing.
about: The controversy over alleged pilferage from Ram temple donations escalated on multiple fronts. The RSS publicly pressed the Shri Ram Janmabhoomi Teerth Kshetra Trust and the state SIT toward "prompt action and correction" — a notable intervention from an organisation that rarely criticises anything in the Ayodhya orbit, and one landing conspicuously ahead of the 2027 Uttar Pradesh assembly elections. BJP MPs and Nitin Nabin echoed the demand. Congress went the other way, demanding a Supreme Court-monitored investigation and asking, pointedly, why enforcement agencies stay silent on the "big thieves" while pursuing smaller figures; K.C. Venugopal wrote directly to the Prime Minister. A crucial Trust meeting was scheduled for Monday to discuss the SIT report, against the backdrop of the resignations of senior temple functionaries Champat Rai and Anil Mishra. A parallel "donation theft" probe was underway at the Badrinath shrine.
left: The temple was sold to the country as the culmination of a decades-long civilisational movement — its crown jewel — and the moment real money began flowing through it, it leaked. The RSS's sudden appetite for "correction" is not conscience; it's damage control, and the timing tells you why — this is the run-up to UP polls, and the scandal threatens the sanctity that gives the whole project its electoral value. Congress's question about why agencies chase small fry while the "big thieves" go untouched cuts to the real issue: selective enforcement operating inside the movement's own showpiece institution.
right: This is precisely accountability in action, and it demolishes the caricature. The RSS and the Trust are confronting the theft openly, an SIT has been ordered, and senior functionaries have resigned rather than the whole thing being buried — that is an institution policing itself. Isolated wrongdoing by individuals does not indict the temple or the movement behind it; the swift, public response is the story, and it's a credit. Congress's demand for a Supreme Court-monitored probe is opportunistic politicisation of devotees' faith.
reality: Large religious trusts running on cash with thin audit trails leak money as a structural feature, not as a moral failing unique to Ayodhya — this is what opaque, high-volume donation systems do everywhere. The RSS spoke because the scandal endangers the brand at precisely the wrong moment, right before UP polls, and being seen to demand a clean-up is the cost of protecting the asset. The SIT and the resignations will surface some names and produce some accountability, and they will stop at the point where the trail threatens anyone whose fall would embarrass the movement. The line between a genuine reckoning and controlled damage limitation sits exactly where the SIT's mandate is allowed to end — and that boundary, not the rhetoric on either side, is the thing to watch.

headline: Punjab Congress Says 'No Rift.' The Twenty MLAs at Channi's House Disagree.
subhead: Warring folds, Channi flexes, and Randhawa meets Amit Shah.
about: Punjab Congress president Amrinder Singh Raja Warring publicly dismissed talk of rebellion in the state unit, declared he is "not in the race" for the chief minister's post, and backed the party high command's authority on leadership questions. His statement came a day after roughly twenty MLAs and former legislators gathered at former CM Charanjit Singh Channi's residence in Rupnagar in an unmistakable show of force, and after another senior leader, Randhawa, met Union Home Minister Amit Shah — a meeting that set off defection speculation. The BJP needled the Congress publicly, asking whether the party would act against the leaders who had rallied behind Channi.
left: The Congress keeps losing states not on ideology but through its own feudal turf wars, and Warring's insistence that "we're united" is papering over a leadership vacuum the high command has proven unable to fill. A show of twenty MLAs at one leader's residence is not unity; it's a faction flexing. Every one of these implosions becomes an open audition for the BJP, which absorbs the defectors and, eventually, the mandate — a self-inflicted tragedy for a party that then asks voters to trust it with government.
right: This is the natural unravelling of a rudderless, dynasty-run party with no vision beyond its own survival, and the drift toward the exit — leaders quietly taking meetings with the Home Minister — is the writing on the wall. Voters can see a Congress consumed by infighting while the alternative offers stability and governance. Warring's public denial of a rift fools no one when the evidence is a mass gathering at a rival leader's home and a senior figure meeting Amit Shah.
reality: This is a contest over patronage and the chief-minister face, not over any question of direction — nobody in that Rupnagar gathering is arguing about policy. The "show of strength" is a price quote held up to the high command, and Randhawa's meeting with Shah is a bargaining chip, not necessarily a defection. Warring publicly folding — declaring himself "not in the race" — signals the high command won this particular round, but the underlying dynamic is unchanged: the faction that can most credibly threaten to walk sets the terms of the next crisis, which rewards disloyalty and guarantees there will be one.

headline: 'Only Talks Can Bring Peace,' Says the Man Who Helped Start the Fire
subhead: At an MLA's funeral, Biren Singh pleads for dialogue from the office that deepened the divide.
about: At the funeral of BJP MLA Y. Vungzagin Valte — who was attacked by a mob in May 2023 and finally died of his injuries in February 2026 — Manipur Chief Minister N. Biren Singh said that only dialogue can bring peace to the state, and expressed regret at not having been with his "friend" when the attack happened, adding that he would work to find a road map to peace. The remarks land against a specific backdrop: Singh had earlier called for a boycott of the ethnic Kuki-Zo group's participation in the state assembly, and his own role in the polarisation of the conflict is contested. So the plea for dialogue comes from a leader whose record on the conflict is itself part of the argument.
left: A chief minister calling for talks at the funeral of an MLA killed on his watch — while having backed an assembly boycott of one of the ethnic communities at the heart of the conflict — is the figure most implicated in the polarisation now presenting himself as the peacemaker. "Only talks can bring peace" rings hollow coming from leadership that helped entrench the divide and then failed to contain the violence for years. The grief may be real, but the positioning is unmistakable.
right: Deep ethnic conflicts genuinely cannot be resolved by imposition from above — the communities themselves have to reach an accommodation, and the chief minister saying so is simply being realistic about the ground truth. Expressing personal grief at the funeral of a fallen colleague is human, not cynical, and reading hypocrisy into a blunt, accurate assessment of an intractable situation is bad-faith spin.
reality: Manipur's conflict has resisted resolution for years, and "only talks can bring peace" is simultaneously an honest assessment and an exit from accountability for a leadership that is implicated in the fracture it is now lamenting. Delivering that line at the funeral of a victim killed on his watch fuses genuine grief with political self-positioning in a way that's almost impossible to disentangle. What's certain is that the state has cast itself as referee to a fire it helped light, and the people living inside the conflict are the ones absorbing the cost of that ambiguity while the road map remains, for now, a phrase at a funeral rather than a policy.

headline: 'I Ignore All Traitors,' Says Mamata. Then Her Finance Minister Walked Out.
subhead: A resignation from the senior ranks confirms the TMC's succession crisis is real.
about: Mamata Banerjee ended weeks of public silence about the Ritabrata-aligned rebel faction inside the Trinamool Congress, saying she "ignores all traitors," accusing the rebels of betraying the party after winning on its symbol, and daring them to join the BJP. She insisted the symbol stays with her. On the same day, one of her senior colleagues — former West Bengal finance minister Chandrima Bhattacharya — resigned from all Trinamool posts and aligned herself with the camp of the Opposition Leader Ritabrata Banerjee. So the leader's defiance and a significant defection landed in the same news cycle, bringing an internal war that had been simmering fully into the open ahead of the state assembly contest.
left: A personality-cult party with no succession mechanism will inevitably fracture into a loyalists-versus-rebels split, and "go join the BJP" is genuinely the only insult a leader-centric outfit has left to throw, because the party has no ideological substance to appeal to instead. The resignation of a former finance minister — not a marginal figure — shows the fracture isn't fringe grumbling; it reaches into the senior ranks.
right: The TMC's implosion lays bare a one-woman party in which dissent is treated as treason, and rebels reading the writing on the wall is a verdict on Mamata's authoritarian grip on the organisation. When a senior aide of the stature of a former finance minister walks out mid-term to join the rebels, that is a damning signal about the health of the party, whatever the leader says.
reality: In a leader-centric party the real, unresolved question is who inherits the machine when the leader is gone, and because nobody has an answer, the contest collapses into loyalty tests and public dares. "Go join the BJP" is theatre — a way of framing defection as betrayal rather than as a rational read of the party's future. The substantive tell is the aide's resignation: a former finance minister moving to the rebel camp is evidence that the succession vacuum is already splintering the organisation from the top, and no amount of "I ignore all traitors" changes the structural fact that a party built entirely around one person has no plan for the day after that person.

headline: 'One Nation, One Election' Sounds Efficient. Here's What It Actually Does.
subhead: Retired judges and former CECs say synchronised polls flatten federalism. The census silence is worse.
about: A conclave in Delhi — retired judges, former bureaucrats, lawyers and civil-society leaders — formally rejected the "One Nation, One Election" proposal, arguing that synchronising national and state elections fractures the federal structure the Constitution is built on. The same gathering questioned the SIR process and its electoral, procedural and caste-related dimensions, and pushed for a caste census; former CEC Ashok Lavasa was among the voices. A related concern raised was the absence of an updated 2027 population census, which complicates the fair allocation of welfare because entitlements are being distributed on the basis of decade-old numbers. So the event bundled three things: a constitutional objection to simultaneous polls, a critique of the roll revision, and an alarm about governing a billion people on stale demographic data.
left: Simultaneous elections centralise power and erase the distinct electoral rhythms of the states, which is federalism-flattening dressed up as administrative efficiency — and it conveniently advantages a dominant national party by nationalising every contest. That a body of retired judges and former CECs is making this case matters, because they know the machinery from the inside. The missing census is the quieter scandal underneath: welfare allocation running on ten-year-old figures systematically shortchanges the populations that have grown fastest, which are disproportionately the poor.
right: Synchronised elections would save enormous public money and end the permanent campaign-mode governance that perpetual state-by-state polling produces, freeing administrations to actually govern. The "federalism" objection is overstated by a class of retired officials reflexively hostile to any structural reform, and simultaneous polls strengthen rather than weaken the democratic calendar by making it coherent. The census concern is real but separable and administrative, not a reason to reject electoral reform.
reality: "One Nation, One Election" is a genuine structural question about who controls the electoral tempo of the country, and the retired-establishment pushback carries real weight precisely because it comes from people who aren't a party to the contest. But the deeper, quieter harm on the table is the un-updated census — allocating welfare for a billion people on the basis of decade-old population numbers is a larger, more concrete injustice than the synchronisation debate, and it gets a fraction of the attention because it lacks the partisan charge. The conclave's real service is putting both on the record; whether either changes is a separate question entirely.

headline: Bihar Promised 100 Fast-Track Courts. Don't Count on the Judges.
subhead: Court announcements and 'double-engine' slogans signal UP 2027 is already underway.
about: Bihar Chief Minister Samrat Choudhary announced the creation of 100 fast-track courts to speed up the disposal of criminal cases, framing it as effective coordination between the judiciary and the executive and holding regular meetings between the two to that end. In parallel, BJP national president Nitin Nabin toured Uttar Pradesh, addressing party workers, calling for a grassroots push and touting the "double-engine government" model ahead of the 2027 UP assembly elections. Both moves are governance-and-organisation signalling in poll-bound states — one a concrete administrative announcement, the other a party-building exercise.
left: Fast-track courts announced on the cusp of an election are governance-as-optics — without the judges, the funding and the physical infrastructure to actually run them, "100 courts" is a headline that may never become a functioning reality. And the "double-engine government" pitch is centralisation branding, a way of arguing that states should hand power upward to align with the Centre, dressed as a governance model.
right: Speeding up a notoriously clogged justice system is exactly the delivery ordinary people want, and institutionalising coordination between the judiciary and executive to clear backlogs is responsible governance, not optics. Building party organisation early and making the case for "double-engine" continuity is simply what a serious political party does ahead of a major election.
reality: Announcing 100 courts is the easy part; staffing them with judges, funding them, and giving them premises is the years-long part that routinely falls short of the press release — and the timing, in a poll-bound Bihar, tells you which of those the announcement is optimised for. The UP groundwork is the more consequential of the two moves, because 2027 is the actual prize, and everything from fast-track-court announcements to "double-engine" slogans is being positioned around it. Whether the courts materialise as functioning institutions or as a number that decorated a campaign is a question that will be answered long after the votes are counted.

headline: Arrested on Monday, Bailed on Tuesday. That Was Always the Point.
subhead: Process-as-punishment runs in all directions across Tamil Nadu politics.
about: A former Tamil Nadu minister was granted bail just a day after being arrested over remarks he made about the chief minister. His party, the TVK, publicly framed the state Police Department as being weaponised for "political games" in a bid to somehow bring down the DMK. Separately and pointing the other way, a DMK MLA, Radhakrishnan, faced the rejection of his anticipatory-bail plea in a parallel case concerning remarks about a CM, and the Greater Chennai police summoned a former minister in an MLA bribery case. So the churn of speech-leads-to-arrest cases is running in multiple directions across Tamil Nadu politics at once.
left: Arresting people over remarks about a chief minister — regardless of which party is doing the arresting — normalises the criminalisation of political speech, and the tit-for-tat pattern of cases running in every direction shows the police being turned into a partisan weapon by whoever holds power. The speed of the bail doesn't undo the point; the arrest itself is the message.
right: If remarks cross the line into defamation or incitement, legal action against them is legitimate, and the fact that bail was granted within a day demonstrates that the system self-corrects rather than persecutes. The "political games" cry is the standard spin of the accused, and cases running in multiple directions actually suggests the law is being applied to speech offences broadly, not selectively targeting one side.
reality: Every ruling party in every state uses the police against its loud opponents, and the volume of the outrage is always calibrated to whose MLA is in the dock — it's loudest when the target is yours and quietest when it's someone else's. The rapid grant of bail strongly suggests the arrest was more about signalling and inconvenience than about sustainable charges, which is the essence of process-as-punishment: the arrest, the booking, the night's detention are themselves the penalty, and the eventual acquittal or bail is an afterthought that arrives after the point has already been made. That the cases run in several directions at once doesn't disprove the pattern; it confirms that this is simply how the tool gets used by everyone who has it.
